title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)
oneLiner: Severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) with profound dehydration, hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg), minimal ketosis, and altered consciousness – a medical emergency in older type 2 diabetes patients with high mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypovolaemic shock** – Look for: systolic BP <90 mmHg, rising lactate, oliguria. **This kills faster than DKA because losses are massive.**  
- **Hypernatraemic neurological decline** – Look for: GCS <12, focal signs, seizures. Beware: hypoglycaemia can be iatrogenic from over-rapid correction – but more commonly the glucose drop lags behind Na correction, causing cerebral oedema.  
- **Thromboembolism** – Look for: unilateral leg swelling, sudden dyspnoea, new ECG changes. HHS is pro-thrombotic; pulmonary embolism is a common preventable cause of death.  
- Immediate action: if any of these present → **call ITU/Senior**, start IV fluids (0.9% saline), order VTE prophylaxis, arrange stat CT head if focal signs.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (days to weeks)  
  - Profound weakness, lethargy  
  - Altered mental state: confusion, drowsiness, or coma (GCS may be 10-14)  
  - Thirst, dry mouth, reduced skin turgor  
  - Often no nausea/vomiting (unlike DKA)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Severe dehydration: sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, reduced capillary refill  
  - Tachycardia, hypotension (postural > supine)  
  - Focal neurological signs (e.g., hemiplegia, aphasia) – may mimic stroke  
  - Minimal Kussmaul breathing (absent or mild tachypnoea)  
  - No ketotic breath
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **DKA** – differentiate by: HHS has almost absent ketones (urine/blood <0.6 mmol/L), glucose >30 mmol/L, pH >7.3. DKA: significant ketosis, acidosis, pH <7.3.  
- **Acute stroke** – check: blood glucose >25 mmol/L + CT head negative? Then consider HHS as a stroke mimic (resolve with fluids).  
- **Sepsis** – check: if no source found, hyperglycaemia + dehydration may be primary. Sepsis can precipitate HHS.  
- **Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy** – check: glucose <3.5 mmol/L; HHS always hyperglycaemic.  
- **Uraemic encephalopathy** – check: urea >30 mmol/L; can coexist if acute kidney injury (common).  
- **Splitter:** Test urine or blood ketones – **negative ketones** (<0.6 mmol/L) and **hypernatraemia** (Na >145 mmol/L) heavily favour HHS over DKA.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Capillary blood glucose (often >30 mmol/L – check meter range)  
  - Blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) – should be <0.6 mmol/L  
  - ECG: screen for silent MI, hyperkalaemia/hypokalaemia, arrhythmia  
  - Urine dipstick: ketones +/–, glucose ++++  
  - NEWS2 score – every 15 minutes initially  
- **Bloods (urgent):**  
  - FBC, U&Es, creatinine, bicarbonate, chloride, lactate  
  - Serum osmolality (measured or calculated: 2 x Na + glucose + urea)  
  - Blood gas (venous): pH, pCO2, bicarbonate, lactate  
  - HbA1c – helps differentiate new vs known diabetes  
  - Infection screen: CRP, blood cultures, chest X-ray  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Chest X-ray – look for pneumonia (common precipitant)  
  - CT head (if focal signs, GCS <13, or after correction fails to improve)  
  - Consider ECG-gated CT if PE suspected  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lactate – if >2 mmol/L, suspect hypoperfusion or sepsis  
  - Insulin, C-peptide (type 1 vs 2) – not emergency  
- **Diagnostic criteria (Joint British Diabetes Societies, JBDS 2022):**  
  - Plasma glucose >30 mmol/L  
  - Calculated osmolality >320 mOsm/kg (or measured >320)  
  - pH >7.3 (or HCO3 >15 mmol/L)  
  - Blood ketones <3 mmol/L (or urine ketones ≤1+)  
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum osmolality** – if >350 mOsm/kg, high risk of obtundation and thromboembolism. Also **ECG** to exclude MI.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride – 1 litre over first 30 min (if hypotensive) or over 1 h if euvolemic. Then assess:  
    - If Na >145 mmol/L: use 0.45% saline (if available) or slower rate.  
    - If Na 135–145: continue 0.9% saline.  
    - If Na <135: treat as DKA (consider cerebral oedema).  
  - **Potassium:** add 20–30 mmol KCl per litre of fluid if K+ <5.5 mmol/L. Aim K+ 4.0–5.0 mmol/L.  
  - **Insulin:** Fixed rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRIII) – 0.05 units/kg/hr (lower than DKA to avoid rapid osmolar shift). **Do not give bolus.**  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Start LMWH immediately (unless risk of bleeding). HHS is very pro-thrombotic.  
  - **Stop precipitating drugs:** e.g., thiazides, steroids, glitazones, SGLT2 inhibitors (can cause DKA, not HHS directly but may contribute).  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV fluids until osmolality stable (<300 mOsm/kg) and patient eating/drinking.  
  - Monitor glucose hourly; aim to lower glucose by ~2–5 mmol/L per hour.  
  - When glucose <15 mmol/L, switch to IV 5% dextrose + insulin sliding scale.  
  - Aim to correct osmolality slowly (no faster than 3 mOsm/kg/hour) to avoid cerebral oedema.  
  - Replace potassium aggressively (often 60–100 mmol K+ in first 24 h).  
  - Look for precipitant: infection (urine, chest), MI, stroke, medication change.  
  - Consider NG tube if GCS <12.  
  - **Transition to subcutaneous insulin** when ketoacidosis resolved, osmolality near normal, and patient eating.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Determine diabetes type: often type 2 – many can be managed on oral agents (metformin, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA) after stabilisation.  
  - Review precipitant (e.g., infection, missed meds) and optimise diabetes care.  
  - Refer to diabetes specialist team.  
- **Default Orders:**  
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline at 250–500 mL/hour (adjust per clinical state)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if eGFR >30) [check local policy]  
  - **Diet:** NBM until GCS >13 and able to swallow safely  
  - **Observation:** q15-minute NEWS2, hourly glucose, 4-hourly U&Es
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride – 1 L stat, then 0.5–1 L/hour based on deficit. Example: 4–6 L in first 8 hours.  
  - **Insulin:** Actrapid® (human soluble) – 0.05 units/kg/hr IV infusion (e.g., 50 units in 50 mL 0.9% saline via syringe pump)  
  - **Potassium chloride:** Add 20–30 mmol in 0.5 L saline if K+ <5.5. If K+ <3.5, give stat 10 mmol IV over 10 min, then continued infusion.  
- **Common alternative:**  
  - If patient develops hypoglycaemia: 50 mL 50% dextrose bolus  
  - Transition to SC insulin: basal-bolus (e.g., detemir/glargine + novorapid) – dose per local protocol  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - Avoid 0.45% saline if Na <135 (risk of hyponatraemia)  
  - Insulin bolus is contraindicated in HHS (risk of rapid glucose drop and cerebral oedema)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - LMWH dose adjustment if eGFR <30 (e.g., enoxaparin 20 mg daily)  
  - Insulin dose no adjustment but monitor for hypoglycaemia  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving IV insulin bolus in HHS** – causes rapid glucose fall, osmotic shift, and potentially fatal cerebral oedema.  
  - **Failing to give potassium with fluids** – leads to hypokalaemia (especially as insulin drives K+ into cells).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Cerebral oedema (rare but devastating)  
  - Hypoglycaemia (from over-rapid correction)  
  - Hypokalaemia / hyperkalaemia  
  - Thromboembolism (PE, DVT, MI, stroke)  
  - Acute kidney injury (often prerenal, but can become ATN)  
  - Infection (pneumonia, UTI, sepsis)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Worsening diabetic control, increased cardiovascular risk  
  - Cognitive impairment after prolonged hyperosmolality  
  - Recurrence if precipitant not addressed
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality: 10–20% (higher than DKA, especially in elderly with comorbidities). Survivors often have prolonged hospital stay.  
- Long-term: most remain on insulin or oral hypoglycaemics; ~30% require insulin permanently.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do a thorough infection screen** (urinalysis, CXR, blood cultures) – infection is the most common precipitant.  
- **Start LMWH immediately** – venous thromboembolism is a leading preventable death.  
- **Use a flow chart / proforma** (e.g., JBDS HHS protocol) to track fluid balance, glucose, Na, osmolality, and potassium hourly.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving over-rapid fluid correction:** careful with elderly – risk of cardiac overload and electrolyte swings.  
- **Failing to check osmolarity frequently:** target slow lowering – rapid drop causes cerebral oedema.  
- **Missing an acute MI:** HHS can present without chest pain; serial ECGs and troponin if any doubt.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New confusion, headache, vomiting, or focal weakness  
  - Blood glucose >30 mmol/L or <4 mmol/L  
  - Signs of DVT/PE: leg swelling, chest pain, breathlessness  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Diabetes specialist nurse/consultant within 1–2 weeks  
  - GP within 1 week to review glucose control, VTE prophylaxis (if still on LMWH)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call Senior / ICU if:**  
  - GCS <12 or falling  
  - Mean arterial pressure <65 mmHg despite 2 L fluids  
  - Serum osmolality >350 mOsm/kg  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - New focal neurology (CT head needed)  
  - Any pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <15 (indicates mixed HHS/DKA)  
- **NEWS2 threshold for immediate escalation:**  
  - Any single score of 3 (e.g., respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <91% on air, heart rate >130, systolic BP <90)  
  - Total NEWS2 ≥7 – call the MET team

rawMarkdown:
## Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)

**One-liner:** Severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) with profound dehydration, hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg), minimal ketosis, and altered consciousness – a medical emergency in older type 2 diabetes patients with high mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypovolaemic shock** – Look for: systolic BP <90 mmHg, rising lactate, oliguria. **This kills faster than DKA because losses are massive.**  
- **Hypernatraemic neurological decline** – Look for: GCS <12, focal signs, seizures. Beware: hypoglycaemia can be iatrogenic from over-rapid correction – but more commonly the glucose drop lags behind Na correction, causing cerebral oedema.  
- **Thromboembolism** – Look for: unilateral leg swelling, sudden dyspnoea, new ECG changes. HHS is pro-thrombotic; pulmonary embolism is a common preventable cause of death.  
- Immediate action: if any of these present → **call ITU/Senior**, start IV fluids (0.9% saline), order VTE prophylaxis, arrange stat CT head if focal signs.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (days to weeks)  
  - Profound weakness, lethargy  
  - Altered mental state: confusion, drowsiness, or coma (GCS may be 10-14)  
  - Thirst, dry mouth, reduced skin turgor  
  - Often no nausea/vomiting (unlike DKA)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Severe dehydration: sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, reduced capillary refill  
  - Tachycardia, hypotension (postural > supine)  
  - Focal neurological signs (e.g., hemiplegia, aphasia) – may mimic stroke  
  - Minimal Kussmaul breathing (absent or mild tachypnoea)  
  - No ketotic breath  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **DKA** – differentiate by: HHS has almost absent ketones (urine/blood <0.6 mmol/L), glucose >30 mmol/L, pH >7.3. DKA: significant ketosis, acidosis, pH <7.3.  
- **Acute stroke** – check: blood glucose >25 mmol/L + CT head negative? Then consider HHS as a stroke mimic (resolve with fluids).  
- **Sepsis** – check: if no source found, hyperglycaemia + dehydration may be primary. Sepsis can precipitate HHS.  
- **Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy** – check: glucose <3.5 mmol/L; HHS always hyperglycaemic.  
- **Uraemic encephalopathy** – check: urea >30 mmol/L; can coexist if acute kidney injury (common).  
- **Splitter:** Test urine or blood ketones – **negative ketones** (<0.6 mmol/L) and **hypernatraemia** (Na >145 mmol/L) heavily favour HHS over DKA.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Capillary blood glucose (often >30 mmol/L – check meter range)  
  - Blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) – should be <0.6 mmol/L  
  - ECG: screen for silent MI, hyperkalaemia/hypokalaemia, arrhythmia  
  - Urine dipstick: ketones +/–, glucose ++++  
  - NEWS2 score – every 15 minutes initially  
- **Bloods (urgent):**  
  - FBC, U&Es, creatinine, bicarbonate, chloride, lactate  
  - Serum osmolality (measured or calculated: 2 x Na + glucose + urea)  
  - Blood gas (venous): pH, pCO2, bicarbonate, lactate  
  - HbA1c – helps differentiate new vs known diabetes  
  - Infection screen: CRP, blood cultures, chest X-ray  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Chest X-ray – look for pneumonia (common precipitant)  
  - CT head (if focal signs, GCS <13, or after correction fails to improve)  
  - Consider ECG-gated CT if PE suspected  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lactate – if >2 mmol/L, suspect hypoperfusion or sepsis  
  - Insulin, C-peptide (type 1 vs 2) – not emergency  
- **Diagnostic criteria (Joint British Diabetes Societies, JBDS 2022):**  
  - Plasma glucose >30 mmol/L  
  - Calculated osmolality >320 mOsm/kg (or measured >320)  
  - pH >7.3 (or HCO3 >15 mmol/L)  
  - Blood ketones <3 mmol/L (or urine ketones ≤1+)  
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum osmolality** – if >350 mOsm/kg, high risk of obtundation and thromboembolism. Also **ECG** to exclude MI.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride – 1 litre over first 30 min (if hypotensive) or over 1 h if euvolemic. Then assess:  
    - If Na >145 mmol/L: use 0.45% saline (if available) or slower rate.  
    - If Na 135–145: continue 0.9% saline.  
    - If Na <135: treat as DKA (consider cerebral oedema).  
  - **Potassium:** add 20–30 mmol KCl per litre of fluid if K+ <5.5 mmol/L. Aim K+ 4.0–5.0 mmol/L.  
  - **Insulin:** Fixed rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRIII) – 0.05 units/kg/hr (lower than DKA to avoid rapid osmolar shift). **Do not give bolus.**  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Start LMWH immediately (unless risk of bleeding). HHS is very pro-thrombotic.  
  - **Stop precipitating drugs:** e.g., thiazides, steroids, glitazones, SGLT2 inhibitors (can cause DKA, not HHS directly but may contribute).  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV fluids until osmolality stable (<300 mOsm/kg) and patient eating/drinking.  
  - Monitor glucose hourly; aim to lower glucose by ~2–5 mmol/L per hour.  
  - When glucose <15 mmol/L, switch to IV 5% dextrose + insulin sliding scale.  
  - Aim to correct osmolality slowly (no faster than 3 mOsm/kg/hour) to avoid cerebral oedema.  
  - Replace potassium aggressively (often 60–100 mmol K+ in first 24 h).  
  - Look for precipitant: infection (urine, chest), MI, stroke, medication change.  
  - Consider NG tube if GCS <12.  
  - **Transition to subcutaneous insulin** when ketoacidosis resolved, osmolality near normal, and patient eating.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Determine diabetes type: often type 2 – many can be managed on oral agents (metformin, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA) after stabilisation.  
  - Review precipitant (e.g., infection, missed meds) and optimise diabetes care.  
  - Refer to diabetes specialist team.  
- **Default Orders:**  
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline at 250–500 mL/hour (adjust per clinical state)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if eGFR >30) [check local policy]  
  - **Diet:** NBM until GCS >13 and able to swallow safely  
  - **Observation:** q15-minute NEWS2, hourly glucose, 4-hourly U&Es  

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride – 1 L stat, then 0.5–1 L/hour based on deficit. Example: 4–6 L in first 8 hours.  
  - **Insulin:** Actrapid® (human soluble) – 0.05 units/kg/hr IV infusion (e.g., 50 units in 50 mL 0.9% saline via syringe pump)  
  - **Potassium chloride:** Add 20–30 mmol in 0.5 L saline if K+ <5.5. If K+ <3.5, give stat 10 mmol IV over 10 min, then continued infusion.  
- **Common alternative:**  
  - If patient develops hypoglycaemia: 50 mL 50% dextrose bolus  
  - Transition to SC insulin: basal-bolus (e.g., detemir/glargine + novorapid) – dose per local protocol  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - Avoid 0.45% saline if Na <135 (risk of hyponatraemia)  
  - Insulin bolus is contraindicated in HHS (risk of rapid glucose drop and cerebral oedema)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - LMWH dose adjustment if eGFR <30 (e.g., enoxaparin 20 mg daily)  
  - Insulin dose no adjustment but monitor for hypoglycaemia  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving IV insulin bolus in HHS** – causes rapid glucose fall, osmotic shift, and potentially fatal cerebral oedema.  
  - **Failing to give potassium with fluids** – leads to hypokalaemia (especially as insulin drives K+ into cells).  

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Cerebral oedema (rare but devastating)  
  - Hypoglycaemia (from over-rapid correction)  
  - Hypokalaemia / hyperkalaemia  
  - Thromboembolism (PE, DVT, MI, stroke)  
  - Acute kidney injury (often prerenal, but can become ATN)  
  - Infection (pneumonia, UTI, sepsis)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Worsening diabetic control, increased cardiovascular risk  
  - Cognitive impairment after prolonged hyperosmolality  
  - Recurrence if precipitant not addressed  

### Prognosis
- Mortality: 10–20% (higher than DKA, especially in elderly with comorbidities). Survivors often have prolonged hospital stay.  
- Long-term: most remain on insulin or oral hypoglycaemics; ~30% require insulin permanently.  

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do a thorough infection screen** (urinalysis, CXR, blood cultures) – infection is the most common precipitant.  
- **Start LMWH immediately** – venous thromboembolism is a leading preventable death.  
- **Use a flow chart / proforma** (e.g., JBDS HHS protocol) to track fluid balance, glucose, Na, osmolality, and potassium hourly.  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving over-rapid fluid correction:** careful with elderly – risk of cardiac overload and electrolyte swings.  
- **Failing to check osmolarity frequently:** target slow lowering – rapid drop causes cerebral oedema.  
- **Missing an acute MI:** HHS can present without chest pain; serial ECGs and troponin if any doubt.  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New confusion, headache, vomiting, or focal weakness  
  - Blood glucose >30 mmol/L or <4 mmol/L  
  - Signs of DVT/PE: leg swelling, chest pain, breathlessness  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Diabetes specialist nurse/consultant within 1–2 weeks  
  - GP within 1 week to review glucose control, VTE prophylaxis (if still on LMWH)  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call Senior / ICU if:**  
  - GCS <12 or falling  
  - Mean arterial pressure <65 mmHg despite 2 L fluids  
  - Serum osmolality >350 mOsm/kg  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - New focal neurology (CT head needed)  
  - Any pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <15 (indicates mixed HHS/DKA)  
- **NEWS2 threshold for immediate escalation:**  
  - Any single score of 3 (e.g., respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <91% on air, heart rate >130, systolic BP <90)  
  - Total NEWS2 ≥7 – call the MET team
